dependencies.description: Requires IP reachability between neighbors. EIGRP uses IP protocol 88 and multicast 224.0.0.10.
dependencies.name: IP connectivity

dependencies.description: All EIGRP neighbors on a segment must share the same autonomous system number to form adjacencies.
dependencies.name: Matching AS number

dependencies.description: Metric weights (K1-K5) must match between EIGRP neighbors. Default: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0 (bandwidth + delay). Mismatch prevents adjacency.
dependencies.name: Matching K-values

dependencies.description: Interfaces must be included in EIGRP via network statements or interface-level EIGRP configuration for neighbors to form on those interfaces.
dependencies.name: EIGRP enabled on interfaces via network statement

failure_modes.description: EIGRP neighbor adjacency fails to establish. No neighbor appears in the EIGRP neighbor table despite both routers being configured for EIGRP on a shared link.

resolutions.action: Verify AS number matches on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols'
Verify AS number matches on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols'
Verify AS number matches on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols'
Verify K-values match on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols' — check metric weights
Verify K-values match on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols' — check metric weights
Verify K-values match on both routers: 'show eigrp protocols' — check metric weights
Check authentication configuration: key chain, key ID, key string
Check authentication configuration: key chain, key ID, key string
Check authentication configuration: key chain, key ID, key string
Check ACLs: look for deny of protocol 88 or 224.0.0.10
Check ACLs: look for deny of protocol 88 or 224.0.0.10
Check ACLs: look for deny of protocol 88 or 224.0.0.10
Verify interface is not passive: 'show eigrp protocols' — check passive interfaces
Verify interface is not passive: 'show eigrp protocols' — check passive interfaces
Verify interface is not passive: 'show eigrp protocols' — check passive interfaces
Ensure network statement covers the interface IP or EIGRP is enabled on the interface
Ensure network statement covers the interface IP or EIGRP is enabled on the interface
Ensure network statement covers the interface IP or EIGRP is enabled on the interface

root_causes.cause: AS number mismatch between neighbors
K-value mismatch (metric weights differ between neighbors)
Authentication mismatch (key chain or key ID)
ACL blocking IP protocol 88 or multicast 224.0.0.10
passive-interface configured on the shared interface

symptoms: Expected adjacency missing on shared segment

failure_modes.description: A route remains in ACTIVE state beyond the active timer (default 3 minutes). EIGRP has sent queries for a lost route but one or more neighbors have not replied. When the active timer expires, the non-responding neighbor relationship is reset, which can cascade and cause widespread instability.

resolutions.action: Configure EIGRP stub on spoke/leaf routers to limit query scope
Configure EIGRP stub on spoke/leaf routers to limit query scope
Configure EIGRP stub on spoke/leaf routers to limit query scope
Implement route summarization at distribution boundaries to contain queries
Implement route summarization at distribution boundaries to contain queries
Implement route summarization at distribution boundaries to contain queries
Check neighbor health: CPU utilization, memory, interface errors
Check neighbor health: CPU utilization, memory, interface errors
Check neighbor health: CPU utilization, memory, interface errors
Verify bidirectional connectivity to the non-responding neighbor
Verify bidirectional connectivity to the non-responding neighbor
Verify bidirectional connectivity to the non-responding neighbor
Consider increasing active timer only as a temporary measure
Consider increasing active timer only as a temporary measure
Consider increasing active timer only as a temporary measure

root_causes.cause: Query propagating too far — no summarization or stub configuration to limit query scope
Slow neighbor not responding to query in time (CPU overload, large topology)
Unidirectional link issue preventing reply from reaching querier
Large topology with many queries overwhelming a neighbor

symptoms: Route stuck in ACTIVE state in 'show eigrp topology active'
Neighbor reset after active timer expiry
Possible cascading neighbor resets in large networks

failure_modes.description: Expected EIGRP-learned routes are not present in the routing table or traffic is taking an unexpected path instead of the optimal EIGRP route.

resolutions.action: Check for route filtering: 'show eigrp protocols' for distribute-lists
Check for route filtering: 'show eigrp protocols' for distribute-lists
Check for route filtering: 'show eigrp protocols' for distribute-lists
Verify K-values match across the EIGRP domain
Verify K-values match across the EIGRP domain
Verify K-values match across the EIGRP domain
Check summarization configuration: 'show eigrp topology' for summary routes
Check summarization configuration: 'show eigrp topology' for summary routes
Check summarization configuration: 'show eigrp topology' for summary routes
Configure variance for unequal-cost load balancing if needed
Configure variance for unequal-cost load balancing if needed
Configure variance for unequal-cost load balancing if needed
Verify metrics: 'show eigrp topology <prefix>' to compare FD and RD
Verify metrics: 'show eigrp topology <prefix>' to compare FD and RD
Verify metrics: 'show eigrp topology <prefix>' to compare FD and RD

root_causes.cause: distribute-list filtering routes inbound or outbound
Mismatched metric K-values causing different path calculations on different routers
Summarization hiding specific routes behind a summary
Variance not configured for unequal-cost load balancing when expected
Offset-list modifying metrics and changing path selection

symptoms: Expected routes not in 'show ip route eigrp'
Traffic taking an unexpected path through the network
Ping or traceroute showing suboptimal path

failure_modes.description: When EIGRP and another IGP (typically OSPF) are mutually redistributed at two or more boundary routers, routes can loop back into EIGRP with a better metric or AD than the native EIGRP route, causing routing loops or suboptimal paths. EIGRP external routes (AD 170) vs EIGRP internal (AD 90) vs OSPF (AD 110) creates a complex AD hierarchy that can be exploited by redistribution loops.

resolutions.action: Use route tags: 'set tag <N>' during redistribution, then 'deny tag <N>' when redistributing back — prevents routes from being re-imported into their source protocol
Use route tags: 'set tag <N>' during redistribution, then 'deny tag <N>' when redistributing back — prevents routes from being re-imported into their source protocol
Use route tags: 'set tag <N>' during redistribution, then 'deny tag <N>' when redistributing back — prevents routes from being re-imported into their source protocol
Adjust AD: consider setting EIGRP external AD lower or matching to prevent OSPF redistributed routes from winning
Adjust AD: consider setting EIGRP external AD lower or matching to prevent OSPF redistributed routes from winning
Adjust AD: consider setting EIGRP external AD lower or matching to prevent OSPF redistributed routes from winning
Apply prefix-lists: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Apply prefix-lists: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Apply prefix-lists: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Configure EIGRP stub on spoke routers to prevent them from becoming redistribution transit points
Configure EIGRP stub on spoke routers to prevent them from becoming redistribution transit points
Configure EIGRP stub on spoke routers to prevent them from becoming redistribution transit points
Reduce redistribution points to a single boundary router where possible
Reduce redistribution points to a single boundary router where possible
Reduce redistribution points to a single boundary router where possible

root_causes.cause: Mutual redistribution at two or more points without loop prevention
No route tags to identify and filter redistributed routes
AD hierarchy: EIGRP internal (90) < OSPF (110) < EIGRP external (170) — redistributed routes from OSPF can have better AD than EIGRP external routes
Missing prefix-list or distribute-list at redistribution boundaries

failure_modes.scenario: Route redistribution loop (mutual redistribution with OSPF/BGP)
symptoms: Route shows as EIGRP external (D EX) when it should be EIGRP internal (D)
'show ip route <prefix>' shows route learned via redistribution with unexpected metric
Suboptimal path — traffic traversing both IGP domains unnecessarily

verify_commands.look_for: Check if route is D (internal), D EX (external/redistributed), or O (OSPF) — unexpected source indicates redistribution issue

failure_modes.description: EIGRP neighbor adjacency repeatedly tears down and re-establishes, causing route instability, topology churn, and potential traffic disruptions during reconvergence.

resolutions.action: Check physical layer: 'show interface' for errors, CRC, carrier transitions
Check physical layer: 'show interface' for errors, CRC, carrier transitions
Check physical layer: 'show interface' for errors, CRC, carrier transitions
Check CPU: 'show processes cpu' for high utilization preventing Hello processing
Check CPU: 'show processes cpu' for high utilization preventing Hello processing
Check CPU: 'show processes cpu' for high utilization preventing Hello processing
Consider BFD for fast failure detection instead of aggressive EIGRP timers
Consider BFD for fast failure detection instead of aggressive EIGRP timers
Consider BFD for fast failure detection instead of aggressive EIGRP timers
Stabilize the underlying transport before tuning EIGRP timers
Stabilize the underlying transport before tuning EIGRP timers
Stabilize the underlying transport before tuning EIGRP timers
Check for L2 issues: STP convergence, LACP flapping on the path
Check for L2 issues: STP convergence, LACP flapping on the path
Check for L2 issues: STP convergence, LACP flapping on the path

root_causes.cause: Hold timer expiry caused by CPU overload on one or both routers
Interface flapping (physical or L2 issue)
Aggressive hello/hold timers without BFD
Packet loss on the link preventing Hellos from being received

symptoms: Short uptime values in 'show eigrp neighbors'
High retransmit count in neighbor table
Route instability and possible traffic blackholes

verify_commands.confirms_if: Confirms neighbor is flapping — short uptime and high retransmits
verify_commands.look_for: Check uptime column for short/resetting values and retransmit count for high values

messages.name: Update
messages.when_sent: On topology change or initial neighbor exchange

messages.description: Asks neighbors for route when no feasible successor is available
messages.name: Query

messages.description: Response to Query with route information
messages.name: Reply

purpose: Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol — advanced distance-vector (DUAL) IGP with fast convergence

states.description: Hello received from neighbor, waiting for full initial topology exchange to complete. Stuck in PENDING indicates a problem with adjacency formation — typically K-value mismatch, AS number mismatch, or authentication failure.
states.name: PENDING

states.description: Neighbor is up and topology exchange is complete. This is the normal steady state for EIGRP neighbors.
states.name: UP

states.description: Route is stable with a valid Feasible Distance (FD) and Reported Distance (RD) calculated. A feasible successor may or may not exist. This is the normal steady state for routes in the EIGRP topology table.
states.name: PASSIVE

states.description: Route has lost its feasible successor and no feasible successor is available in the topology table. EIGRP is actively querying neighbors for an alternate path. Stuck in ACTIVE (SIA) indicates a neighbor is not responding to queries — this is a serious condition that leads to neighbor resets.
states.name: ACTIVE

timers.description: Interval between Hello packets sent to neighbors
timers.name: hello-interval

timers.default_rfc: 15s (3x hello for high-bw), 180s (3x hello for low-bw)
timers.description: Time without a Hello before declaring neighbor dead
timers.name: hold-time

timers.description: Stuck-in-Active (SIA) timer — maximum time a route can remain in ACTIVE state before the non-responding neighbor is reset
timers.name: active-timer
timers.tuning_advice: Increase in very large networks to allow queries to propagate; better to use stub configuration to limit query scope

timers.description: Retransmission timeout for reliable transport — controls how long EIGRP waits before retransmitting an unacknowledged reliable packet
timers.name: RTO

transitions.trigger: All topology updates received and acknowledged
Hold timer expired or goodbye message received

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Possible K-value mismatch, AS number mismatch, or authentication failure
transitions.trigger: Hold timer expired before topology exchange completes
Best route lost and no feasible successor available

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Stuck-in-Active (SIA) — neighbor not responding to query
transitions.trigger: Reply received from all queried neighbors (new successor found)

notes.note: EIGRP available in classic and named mode. Named mode recommended: 'router eigrp <name>' with address-family. Wide metrics supported.
EIGRP supported but not commonly used on IOS-XR platforms. Configured under 'router eigrp <AS>' with address-family. IS-IS or OSPF preferred as IGP on IOS-XR.
EIGRP requires 'feature eigrp'. Supports named EIGRP mode. Wide metrics enabled by default (64-bit metrics) which changes metric calculation.
